---
title: "GitHub Contribution Workflow"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Overview

This page documents the GitHub issue-and-PR workflow used during this project. It serves as a personal
reference for contributing to a repository — either your own or someone else's — following open-source
conventions.

The workflow modelled here is **issue → branch → fix → PR → close**. Every change is traceable back
to a logged reason.

---

## Step 1 — Find or raise an issue

Before touching code, the problem is recorded as a **GitHub Issue**. Issues are the paper trail:
they explain *why* a change was made and link the fix back to the motivation.

```powershell
# Create an issue from the command line using the GitHub CLI (gh)
gh issue create \
  --title "Short, specific description of the problem" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Description
What is broken and where.

## Expected behaviour
What should happen.

## Actual behaviour
What actually happens, including error output or screenshots.

## Steps to reproduce
Numbered list so someone else can confirm the bug.

## Suggested fix
Optional — your best guess at the solution.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Checkbox list of what "done" looks like
EOF
)"
```

**In a project you don't own**, you'd browse to the repo on GitHub, click **Issues → New issue**, and
fill in the same information. The maintainer triages it, and if they accept it you get assigned or
invited to submit a PR.

---

## Step 2 — Create a branch

Never commit fixes directly to `main`. Create a named branch that references the issue.

```powershell
# Convention: type/short-description
# Common prefixes: fix/, feat/, docs/, refactor/, chore/
git checkout -b fix/tests-qmd-dynamic-rendering
```

Naming conventions matter in collaborative repos — maintainers scan branch names to understand intent
before reading code.

**In someone else's repo** you would first **fork** the repository (top-right button on GitHub), clone
*your fork*, then create the branch there:

```powershell
# Fork happens on GitHub, then:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/their-repo.git
cd their-repo
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL-OWNER/their-repo.git
git checkout -b fix/short-description
```

---

## Step 3 — Make the fix

Edit files, run tests, confirm the fix works. Keep commits small and focused.

```powershell
# Stage specific files (never git add -A blindly)
git add quarto/tests.qmd

# Write a commit message that explains WHY, not just WHAT
git commit -m "Load test-results.json dynamically in tests.qmd

Previously the page was static markdown with hardcoded checkmarks.
Now a Python code cell loads the JSON on every render so results
reflect the actual last test run.

Refs #1, #2"
```

Good commit messages follow the pattern:

```
Short imperative summary (≤72 chars)

Blank line, then a paragraph explaining the motivation — what was
wrong, why this approach was chosen, any tradeoffs.

Refs #N  ← links the commit to the issue in GitHub's UI
```

---

## Step 4 — Push and open a Pull Request

```powershell
# Push branch to remote (origin = your fork or your own repo)
git push -u origin fix/tests-qmd-dynamic-rendering

# Open a PR from the command line
gh pr create \
  --title "Fix: render test results dynamically from JSON" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
- Replace hardcoded markdown in `tests.qmd` with Python code cells that load `test-results.json`
- Fix pytest output path so the JSON lands where the `.qmd` expects it

## Test plan
- [ ] Run `pytest tests/test_setup.py --json-report --json-report-file=test-results.json`
- [ ] Render `quarto/tests.qmd` and confirm results table appears
- [ ] Verify failed/skipped tests show error messages

Closes #1
Closes #2
EOF
)"
```

`Closes #N` is a **magic keyword** — when the PR is merged, GitHub automatically closes the linked
issue. Other valid keywords: `Fixes #N`, `Resolves #N`.

**In someone else's repo**, you'd open the PR *from your fork's branch* targeting the upstream
`main`. GitHub shows a "Compare & pull request" banner automatically after you push.

---

## Step 5 — Review and merge

In a collaborative project a maintainer reviews the PR, leaves comments, and requests changes if
needed. You push additional commits to the same branch — they appear in the PR automatically.

```powershell
# If the reviewer requests a change:
# 1. Make the edit
git add quarto/tests.qmd
git commit -m "Address review: handle missing test-results.json gracefully"
git push   # same branch, PR updates automatically
```

Once approved:

- The maintainer clicks **Squash and merge** (or Merge, depending on repo policy)
- The linked issues close automatically
- The branch can be deleted

```powershell
# Clean up local branch after merge
git checkout main
git pull
git branch -d fix/tests-qmd-dynamic-rendering
```

---

## Quick-reference command card

| Action | Command |
|--------|---------|
| Create issue | `gh issue create --title "..." --body "..."` |
| List open issues | `gh issue list` |
| View issue | `gh issue view 3` |
| Create branch | `git checkout -b fix/description` |
| Push branch | `git push -u origin fix/description` |
| Open PR | `gh pr create --title "..." --body "..."` |
| View PR status | `gh pr status` |
| List PRs | `gh pr list` |
| Merge PR | `gh pr merge --squash` |
| Close issue manually | `gh issue close 3` |

---

## CI — Continuous Integration with GitHub Actions

Every push to this repo triggers an automated test run on GitHub's servers. This is called
**Continuous Integration (CI)** — the idea that code is integrated (tested) continuously, not just
before a release.

The workflow lives at `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]   # every branch, every push
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]   # and every PR targeting main

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest   # clean virtual machine, nothing pre-installed
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4          # download the code
      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5        # install uv
      - run: uv sync                       # install Python dependencies
      - run: pytest tests/test_ingest.py -v --json-report --json-report-file=test-results.json
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4   # save test-results.json forever on this run
```

**What it proves:** the tests pass on a clean machine, not just on a local laptop. Every commit in
the git history has a green or red badge — a permanent, timestamped record of whether the code
was working at that point.

**Why `test_setup.py` is excluded from CI:** those tests check for a running Ollama instance
(`localhost:11434`), which doesn't exist on GitHub's servers. They remain as local smoke tests.
`test_ingest.py` uses mocks for Ollama and ChromaDB, so it runs cleanly anywhere.

---

## Branch Rulesets — enforcing the review process

A **ruleset** is a policy applied to a branch. For this project, `main` is protected by a ruleset
that requires CI to pass before any merge is allowed.

**How to set it up:** Settings → Rules → Rulesets → New branch ruleset

- **Target:** `main`
- **Enforcement:** Active
- **Rule:** Require status checks to pass → add the `test` check from `ci.yml`

**Effect:** the merge button on GitHub is physically blocked until the CI check is green. This
means every commit that lands on `main` has been tested. It also forces a deliberate human
decision to merge — you can't accidentally push a broken change.

---

## Human sign-off — creating an evidence trail

The CI check proves the code works. The human comment proves the code was *reviewed*. For the
meta-AI strand of this project, it matters that Alan's approval is clearly recorded separately
from Claude's actions.

**Pattern used in this project:**

1. Claude opens a PR with a comment header:
   `> **Generated by Claude Sonnet 4.6 — actioned on Alan McDonagh's approval**`
2. CI runs automatically — green tick or red cross appears on the PR
3. Alan reviews the Actions output and leaves a comment: *"Reviewed CI output — approving merge"*
4. Alan merges — the merge is timestamped under Alan's GitHub account

This produces an audit trail where every actor (Claude, CI, Alan) is distinguishable and
every action is timestamped.

---

## Issue closing workflow (mandatory for this project)

Every issue must pass through four steps before it is closed:

| Step | What happens |
|------|-------------|
| 1. Tests pass | `pytest` green locally and in CI |
| 2. Quarto demo | A `.qmd` page shows the feature working with live code output |
| 3. Nav updated | The page is registered in `quarto/_quarto.yml` |
| 4. Close with comment | GitHub issue closed with AI attribution header + summary of what was done |

The closing comment always includes:

```
> **Generated by Claude Sonnet 4.6 — actioned on Alan McDonagh's approval**

What was implemented, which tests cover it, which Quarto page demonstrates it.
```

---

## This project's issues raised via this workflow

| Issue | Title | Status |
|-------|-------|--------|
| [#1](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/1) | Ingest: Load course PDFs with PyPDFLoader | Closed — superseded by CSV pivot |
| [#2](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/2) | Ingest: Chunk documents with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter | Closed — implemented in `rag/ingest.py` |
| [#18](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/18) | tests.qmd renders static markdown instead of live results | Closed via PR |
| [#19](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/19) | test-results.json written to wrong path | Closed via PR |
| [#22](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/22) | Switch knowledge base from course PDFs to CBI speeches corpus | Closed — implemented |
| [#24](https://github.com/almac09/private-rag-assistant/issues/24) | Add GitHub Actions CI workflow | Closed via PR #26 |
